body:
> Sometimes, we'll grab query parameters directly from the incoming request, rather than populating them in a struct.
> 
> For example, we might do something like this in our code:

``` go
tagType := req.URL.Query().Get("type")
```

> where req is a typical http.Request pointer.
> 
> I'd like to somehow add a swagger parameter notation directly to that piece of code.
> However, the only way I found to create swagger parameters for that situation is to create a struct and add the parameter into the struct, and add the appropriate go-swagger annotations.
> 
> Is there a way to add parameters without creating a struct first?

At this moment it’s not possible to add a comment to describe a parameter type. It’s not set in stone if that’s the right thing to do or not (so not opposed to changing that). However the reasoning is that I wanted to leverage the go type systems as much as possible and only use comments for things the go type system doesn’t allow to express very well. 

As a workaround you can do this (not very elegant though)

``` go
tag := struct{ /* swagger:parameter type */ Type string }{ Type: req.URL.Query().Get(“type”) } 
```

Currently the library supports generating parameters from an operation by analyzing a struct definition for its type and naming information. Perhaps it would be good to allow for defining the type and naming information as part of the comment annotation.

## Problem statement

I would like to use [Dredd](https://github.com/apiaryio/dredd) to test the generated Swagger against the API. The problem is I'm using path parameters which required a default or an example by Dredd, but since it's a required field, I have to use an example value. I don't think Swagger can generate the vendor extension I need because when I add `// x-example: USERID` to the struct, it will remove the "x-" in the spec which is not the behavior I need.

## Swagger specification

This is the spec it generates with `example` even thought I specify `x-example` in the Go code annotation.

```json
...
        "summary": "Delete a user.",
        "operationId": "UserDestroy",
        "parameters": [
          {
            "type": "string",
            "example": "USERID",
            "x-go-name": "UserID",
            "name": "user_id",
            "in": "path",
            "required": true
          }
        ],
...
```

This is the spec I need with `x-example`:

```json
...
        "summary": "Delete a user.",
        "operationId": "UserDestroy",
        "parameters": [
          {
            "type": "string",
            "x-example": "USERID",
            "x-go-name": "UserID",
            "name": "user_id",
            "in": "path",
            "required": true
          }
```

body:
## Problem statement
To provide some context, Our system is based on a micro-service architecture. And services communicate through HTTP-based SDK. All SDKs have some request and response definitions. We use go-swagger to generate docs for all of our services.

We found an issue caused by name conflicts between service and its dependencies. For example, serviceA has a dependency on another service, serviceB. ServiceB defined a request struct `ResultDownloadRequest` in its SDK. ServiceA uses this request struct to send a request to serviceB. However, serviceA also defined a request struct named `ResultDownloadRequest`. The behavior we observed is that every time we run`swagger generate spec -m -b ./src -o ./docs/swagger.json`, it would generate different results. It would pick up a definition from either serviceA or serviceB randomly. 

We can certainly change the request name to walk around the issue, but it also feels bad that it would pick a random one without throwing an error, which would end up with inconsistent API doc without noticing. And it's not very easy to avoid name conflicts across all of our services. Not to mention that we have to do a manual check on if we have name conflicts within not only the current service but also all of its dependencies.

I'm wondering if there's a better way to avoid this kind of name conflicts. Or if we can set any kind of flag to indicate the priority maybe?

## Environment
swagger version: 0.16.0
go version: go1.10.3 darwin/amd64
OS:  OSX

## Problem statement

Sub-types are not emitted for a discriminated type when using go annotations to generate an OpenAPI spec.

## Annotated go

### `openapi.go`

```go
// From https://goswagger.io/use/spec/discriminated.html

// TeslaCar is a tesla car
//
// swagger:model
type TeslaCar interface {
	// The model of tesla car
	//
	// discriminator: true
	// swagger:name model
	Model() string

	// AutoPilot returns true when it supports autopilot
	// swagger:name autoPilot
	AutoPilot() bool
}

// The ModelS version of the tesla car
//
// swagger:model modelS
type ModelS struct {
	// swagger:allOf com.tesla.models.ModelS
	TeslaCar
	// The edition of this Model S
	Edition string `json:"edition"`
}

// The ModelX version of the tesla car
//
// swagger:model modelX
type ModelX struct {
	// swagger:allOf com.tesla.models.ModelX
	TeslaCar
	// The number of doors on this Model X
	Doors int32 `json:"doors"`
}

// Add a reference to tie TeslaCar into the type definitions hierarchy...

// swagger:response carResponse
type CarResponse struct {
	// in: body
	Body struct {
		Result  int
		Vehicle TeslaCar
	}
}
```

### `routes.go`

```go
...
	// swagger:route GET /car GetCar
	//
	// Gets a Tesla car
	//
	//   Produces:
	//   - application/json
	//
	//   Responses:
	//     200: carResponse
	router.Handle("/", makeCarHandler()).Methods("GET")
...
```

## Generated Swagger Specification

```yaml
basePath: /
consumes:
- application/json
definitions:
  TeslaCar:
    description: TeslaCar is a tesla car
    discriminator: model
    properties:
      autoPilot:
        description: AutoPilot returns true when it supports autopilot
        type: boolean
        x-go-name: AutoPilot
      model:
        description: The model of tesla car
        type: string
        x-go-name: Model
    type: object
    x-go-package: github.com/foo/bar
info:
  description: Package main Car API
  version: "1.0"
paths:

      description: Gets a job document
      operationId: GetRoot
      produces:
      - application/json

        "200":
          $ref: '#/responses/carResponse'
produces:
- application/json
responses:
  carResponse:
    schema:
      properties:
        Result:
          format: int64
          type: integer
        Vehicle:
          $ref: '#/definitions/TeslaCar'
      type: object
schemes:
- http
- https
swagger: "2.0"
```

## Steps to reproduce

1. Create new go project with the above code.
1. Run `swagger generate spec -o swagger.yaml`

## Note

Running the generate command with `-m/--scan-models` does force the subtypes to be emitted in the `definitions` array. However, this comes with the unfortunate side-effect of also generating a model for every `swagger:response` in `definitions` as well (see example below).

I say unfortunate since having to hand edit/cleanup the resulting spec breaks our CI/CD automation.

```yaml
definitions:
  CarResponse:
    properties:
      Body:
        description: 'in: body'
        properties:
          Result:
            format: int64
            type: integer
          Vehicle:
            $ref: '#/definitions/TeslaCar'

    type: object

        type: string

    type: object
    x-go-package: github.com/foo/bar

    allOf:
    - $ref: '#/definitions/TeslaCar'
    - properties:

          description: The edition of this Model S

    description: The ModelS version of the tesla car
    x-class: com.tesla.models.ModelS
    x-go-name: ModelS

    - $ref: '#/definitions/TeslaCar'
    - properties:
```

I encountered this while I was trying to consume Swagger spec generated through go-swagger into AWS API Gateway.
AWS API Gateway adds some extensions, one of them being _x-amazon-apigateway-integration_

```
x-amazon-apigateway-integration:
```

```
# Authentication
Clients should contact the api first. If the api requires authentication it will return a `401 Unauthorized`
response with a `WWW-Authenticate` header detailing how to authenticate to this api.

The api will first return this response:

``
HTTP/1.1 401 Unauthorized
Server: nginx/1.17.5
Date: Fri, 06 Dec 2019 17:40:32 GMT
Content-Type: application/json; charset=utf-8
Content-Length: 79
Connection: keep-alive
Api-Version: 3.2.0
Www-Authenticate: Bearer realm="https://example.com/oauth/token",scope="example:read"

{"status":"error","error":"access to the requested resource is not authorized"}
``

Note the HTTP Response Header indicating the auth challenge:

``
Www-Authenticate: Bearer realm="https://example.com/oauth/token",scope="example:read"
``

	Schemes: https
	BasePath: /v1
	Version: 1.0.0

	Consumes:
	- application/json

swagger:meta
```

Now currently the `Api-Version` gets parsed as the info `Version` and stops the rest of the comment parsing.

## Solution

Update the regular expressions for meta fields to check for space or start of line first.
Example:
```
^\s*[Vv]ersion\p{Zs}*:\p{Zs}*(.+)$
```
This way the version has to start at beginning of line or with spaces and other characters will invalidate it.

I can update all the meta fields regex and submit a pull request if you think this is a good solution.

body:
## Problem statement

Trying to get a map with non-string keys serialized in a spec does not seem to be working quite consistently. I've read the FAQ entry saying maps non-string keys are not supported in bodies, and #977 -- this is somewhat related to it. Anyway it seems to be supported to some extent after all.

## Swagger specification
```go
package main

type Thing struct {
        Value uint64
}

type Key struct {
        V1 string
        V2 string
}

// swagger:response
type Try1 struct {
        Field1 string
        Field2 map[Key]*Thing
}

// swagger:response
// in: body
type Try2 struct {
        Field1 string
        Field2 map[Key]*Thing
}

// swagger:response
type Try3 struct {
        // in: body
        Field1 string
        // in: body
        Field2 map[Key]*Thing
}

// swagger:response
type Try4 struct {
        // in: body
        Data struct {
                Field1 string
                Field2 map[Key]*Thing
        }
}
```

Produced spec:

```yaml
definitions:
  Thing:
    properties:
      Value:
        format: uint64
        type: integer
    type: object
    x-go-package: _/tmp/x
paths: {}
responses:
  Try1:
    headers:
      Field1:
        type: string
      Field2: {}
    schema:
      additionalProperties:
        $ref: '#/definitions/Thing'
      type: object
  Try2:
    headers:
      Field1:
        type: string
      Field2: {}
    schema:
      additionalProperties:
        $ref: '#/definitions/Thing'
      type: object
  Try3:
    schema:
      additionalProperties:
        $ref: '#/definitions/Thing'
      type: object
  Try4:
    schema:
      properties:
        Field1:
          type: string
        Field2: {}
      type: object
swagger: "2.0"
```

## Steps to reproduce

Try1 is my first simplistic attempt. additionalProperties is in the schema, but Field1 and Field2 end up in Headers.

To overcome the unwanted placement, Try2 adds a "in: body". But that does not have any effect.

Try3 is another attempt to overcome it, by adding "in: body" to both struct members. This makes a difference, but both properties disappear from Headers, without appearing in schema.

Try4 is a different approach, it's more in line with the go-swagger examples. Now Field1 and Field2 are where I like to see them, but additionalProperties have disappeared.

The yaml I'm trying to achieve for this is
```yaml
TryX:
    schema:
      properties:
        Field1:
          type: string
        Field2:
          additionalProperties:
            $ref: '#/definitions/Thing'
          type: object
      type: object
```

Key findings:
- Generating specs for maps with non-string keys appears to be possible in some scenarios after all, as witnessed by Try1, Try2, and Try3 above
- If I change the map key to a string, Try4 produces the result I want. I'd rather not do that though, it would be an unwanted change to the underlying model (which serializes fine through use of Text(Un)Marshaler). Also the extra inner wrapper struct (Data in the Try4 example above) would not be desirable, but I guess I could live with it.

## Environment
swagger version: current git master
go version: 1.14
OS:   Linux

## Problem statement

It's already possible to use a workflow like `swagger generate spec --scan-models` + `swagger generate model --existing-models` to output a full model (`struct`) including its implemented validation methods.

However, if we already start with a `struct` + swagger annotations/comments in the first place and are *only interested in auto-generating its validation methods* (in the same go package as the original `struct` from which we have generated the swagger-spec), we currently have to overwrite templates explicitly (`--allow-template-override --template-dir`) to gain such a workflow. Otherwise the emitted struct and the original struct (same name) collide. 

I propose to add an additional flag to `swagger generate model` called `--only-model-validations` which should effectively fully disable the struct emits in the following templates:
* [/generator/templates/schema.gotmpl: Lines 1-76](https://github.com/go-swagger/go-swagger/blob/master/generator/templates/schema.gotmpl#L1-L76)
* [/generator/templates/model.gotmpl: Line 3, 12, 20-21, 23-24](https://github.com/go-swagger/go-swagger/blob/master/generator/templates/model.gotmpl)

Is this a workflow this project is interested to support?

go version: 1.14.2

## Problem statement

A specification that includes a response with an array of objects can be used successfully to generate server stubs.  However, a spec cannot then be generated from the resulting server.  I did not find a way to modify the server code or tags to enable the "generate spec" command to succeed.

The issue is reproducible with the example at https://goswagger.io/tutorial/todo-list.html and with the simple case below.

## Swagger specification

```
swagger: '2.0'
info:
  description: |-
    Test
  version: 1.0.0
  title: test
consumes:
- application/json
produces:
- application/json
paths:
  /:
    get:
      parameters: [
        ]
      responses:
        200:
          description: Success
          schema:
            type: array
            items:
              $ref: '#/definitions/Result'
definitions:
  Result:
    type: object
    properties:
      id:
        type: string
        readOnly: true
      name:
        type: string
```

## Steps to reproduce

1. Copy the spec above into foo.yaml.
1. swagger generate server -A foo -f [...]/foo.yaml -t [...]/foo

body:
## Problem statement

I have generated a swagger specification from annotated go code (with `swagger generate spec`). However, I miss how to validate the API input in runtime to enforce that the input comply with the schema.
I considered 2 approaches:
a) Include validator tags in the go structs. However, these validator tags are ignored by the spec generator. I wouldn't like to work in parallel in the validation tags and the annotated go code because it is error prone and it is probably that they finally differ.
b) Read the json schemas from the spec and apply the appropriate json schema to the input. In this case, I'm not sure how to process the spec to read the json schemas.

I guess that if I start from the swagger spec and I generate the server code, it will include this validators. However, I would prefer to go in the opposite direction: I develop the code and the tool generates the swagger spec.

Is there any recommendation to validate the model of an API request when using `swagger generate spec`?

## Swagger specification

This is just a fragment of the swagger specification generate from the annotated code:

```
definitions:
  UserUpdateBody:
    properties:
      name:
        minLength: 1
        type: string
        x-go-name: Name
    required:
    - name
    type: object
    x-go-package: github.com/...
paths:
  /users/{userID}:
    put:
      operationId: updateUser
      parameters:
      - description: User identifier
        in: path
        name: userID
        required: true
        type: string
        x-go-name: UserID
      - description: User information to create or update a user
        in: body
        name: Body
        required: true
        schema:
          $ref: '#/definitions/UserUpdateBody'
      responses:
        "200":
          $ref: '#/responses/userInfoResponse'
swagger: "2.0"
```

I would like that if I send a request to updateUser operation with a JSON body that does not contain the required parameter `name` or if `name` is empty, the server generates a 400 error response with information about the invalid request. Although I could create the code or middleware to perform the validation, I'm not sure how to validate it using the JSON schema of swagger spec.

Include an embedded struct in a response, label it with `swagger:allOf`, and expect to see a `$ref` in the generated schema.

## Actual Behavior
The fields are listed as properties

## Swagger specification

Note the `allOf` section for `userResponse`

This is the actual generated spec:

```yaml
responses:
  baseResponse:
    description: ""
    headers:
      error:
        description: An error message describing what went wrong
        type: string
      ok:
        description: Whether the request was successful or not
        type: boolean
  userResponse:
    description: ""
    schema:
      allOf:
      - properties:
          error:
            description: An error message describing what went wrong
            type: string
          ok:
            description: Whether the request was successful or not
            type: boolean
        type: object
      - properties:
          user:
            $ref: '#/definitions/User'
        type: object
```

```yaml
responses:
  baseResponse:
    description: ""
    headers:
      error:
        description: An error message describing what went wrong
        type: string
      ok:
        description: Whether the request was successful or not
        type: boolean
  userResponse:
    description: ""
    schema:

      - $ref: '#/responses/baseResponse'
      - properties:
          user:
            $ref: '#/definitions/User'
```

```go
}
```

body:
## Problem statement

Getting the following error when directly or indirectly importing the `github.com/grpc-ecosystem/grpc-gateway/v2/protoc-gen-openapiv2/options` package:
```
classifier: unknown swagger annotation "extendee" 
```

## Swagger specification

2.0

## Steps to reproduce

Run the following command:
```
swagger generate spec -o swagger.json
```
On a go package with a single file like:
```
package swagtest

import (
	_ "github.com/grpc-ecosystem/grpc-gateway/v2/protoc-gen-openapiv2/options"
)
```

## Environment
swagger version: v0.30.4  
go version: go1.19.5 
OS: linux/amd

## Root Cause

Here's the problematic line in the `github.com/grpc-ecosystem/grpc-gateway/v2/protoc-gen-openapiv2/options` package: https://github.com/grpc-ecosystem/grpc-gateway/blob/6adb6b499405b854abfe4ea19e60befc4231eb3e/protoc-gen-openapiv2/options/annotations.pb.go#L195

Here's the line:
```
	0,  // 0: grpc.gateway.protoc_gen_openapiv2.options.openapiv2_swagger:extendee -> google.protobuf.FileOptions 
```

The problem is that the regular expression for matching swagger annotations (`rxSwaggerAnnotation`) is matching the entire `"grpc.gateway.protoc_gen_openapiv2.options.openapiv2_swagger:extendee"` string above:

Here's the `rxSwaggerAnnotation` definition: https://github.com/go-swagger/go-swagger/blob/54644101dc9870a16814f8b18d0cb99e33619a17/codescan/regexprs.go#L31

While this could be seen as a problem on the `grpc-gateway` side, according to the goswagger annotation syntax documentation (https://goswagger.io/use/spec.html#annotation-syntax), the annotation prefix just `swagger:...` and in all documentation examples, the `swagger:` annotations are prefixed by a space. So it seems like a problem on the goswagger side. 

## Possible Fix
If I add a `\b`, the problem doesn't happen:
```
	rxSwaggerAnnotation  = regexp.MustCompile(`\bswagger:([\p{L}\p{N}\p{Pd}\p{Pc}]+)`)
```
`\b` is documented in https://pkg.go.dev/regexp/syntax as `at ASCII word boundary (\w on one side and \W, \A, or \z on the other)`, so when added to the beginning of `rxSwaggerAnnotation` it prevents it from matching the `"...openapiv2_swagger:extended"` string.

I don't know if adding `\b` would be the right solution (it doesn't work as Unicode boundary, for example; here's an article with an alternative albeit very incomplete option: https://shiba1014.medium.com/regex-word-boundaries-with-unicode-207794f6e7ed).

I also don't know what would be the broader implications of adding `\b` to the regexes, e.g. if it could fail existing 

## Workaround
Exclude the package:
```
swagger generate ... -x github.com/grpc-ecosystem/grpc-gateway/v2/protoc-gen-openapiv2/options
```

body:
Additional properties are lost when generating spec.

## Steps to reproduce

first generate a model using a spec that contains additional properties:

```yaml
swagger: "2.0"
info:
  version: "1.0.0"
  title: "Test"

paths: {}
definitions:
  test_additional_properties:
    type: object
    properties:
      field1:
        type: string
    additionalProperties:
      type: number
```
it results in the following model:

```go
// TestAdditionalProperties test additional properties
//
// swagger:model test_additional_properties
type TestAdditionalProperties struct {

	// field1
	Field1 string `json:"field1,omitempty"`

	// test additional properties
	TestAdditionalProperties map[string]float64 `json:"-"`
}
```

when this model is scanned back with generate spec, the additionalProperties are lost:

```yaml
definitions:
    test_additional_properties:
        description: TestAdditionalProperties test additional properties
        properties:
            field1:
                description: field1
                type: string
                x-go-name: Field1
        type: object
        x-go-name: TestAdditionalProperties
        x-go-package: github.com/go-swagger/go-swagger/test_additional_prop/models
paths: {}
swagger: "2.0"
```

## Fix proposal

Annotate additional properties fileds with a special comment, for example like this (similar to examples in https://goswagger.io/use/spec/model.html):

```go
type TestAdditionalProperties struct {
	// test additional properties
        // Additional properties
	TestAdditionalProperties map[string]float64 `json:"-"`
}
```

Then the codescan will check for this comment and will create the correct spec.

## Environment
swagger version: v0.30.5-25-g10db6e00
go version: go1.18.3 linux/amd64
OS: Ubuntu 20.04

## Problem statement
The docs on [this page](https://goswagger.io/use/spec/response.html) provide an example of how to produce a swagger response spec from annotations. However running `swagger generate spec -o swagger.yaml` does not produce the example output.

## Swagger specification
I've copy pasted the example annotation in a test file in package main:
```go
package main

// A ValidationError is an error that is used when the required input fails validation.
// swagger:response validationError
type ValidationError struct {
	// The error message
	// in: body
	Body struct {
		// The validation message
		//
		// Required: true
		// Example: Expected type int
		Message string
		// An optional field name to which this validation applies
		FieldName string
	}
}
```

## Steps to reproduce
Output:
```yaml
paths: {}
responses:
    validationError:
        description: A ValidationError is an error that is used when the required input fails validation.
swagger: "2.0"
```

What it should be:
```yaml
---
responses:
  validationError:
    description: A ValidationError is an error that is used when the required input fails validation.
    schema:
      type: object
      description: The error message
      required:
      - Message
      properties:
        Message:
          type: string
          description: The validation message
          example: Expected type int
        FieldName:
          type: string
          description: an optional field name to which this validation applies
```
Notably, the `schemas` and the `properties` fields are missing. Am I calling this incorrectly here?
## Environment
swagger version: `v0.30.5`
go version: `1.21.5 darwin/arm64`
OS:  macOS 12.6

Currently, `go-swagger` is only considering comments as part of `GenDecl` nodes, and not of the `TypeSpec` nodes themselves. This means that for code like

```go
type (

	EnumType string
)
```

Currently, only basic information like title and description is being parsed and used for typed primitives referenced by top-level models.
